page: Vultr vc2-8c-32gb is a General Purpose virtual server belonging to the Cloud Compute family. It features an Intel x86_64 processor configured with 8 shared vCPUs, derived from 4 physical cores with 2 threads per core, operating at a clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The system is equipped with 32.0 GB of system memory, providing 4.0 GB of RAM per core. Storage is bundled as a 640 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No GPU hardware is present on this configuration.

Performance benchmarks reflect the shared CPU architecture and moderate clock speed of this instance. Single-core processing is a bottleneck, with stress-ng single-core performance and gzip compression benchmarks falling into the bottom 10% tier. However, multi-core execution scales to average levels, as demonstrated by a stress-ng multi-core score of 6282.15 and a PassMark CPU score of 7986.55. Memory read bandwidth is stable at 516.53 GB/sec for small cached blocks, but large-block write bandwidth drops to a poor 14.02 GB/sec. Database operations and static web serving throughput are relatively weak, though Redis SET operations maintain an average tier of 7.55 million operations per second.

Given the shared CPU allocation, this instance offers a cost-effective resource density for workloads that require a balanced memory-to-vCPU ratio without needing sustained, dedicated single-core compute power. The 640 GB NVMe SSD provides ample local storage capacity for non-critical database replicas, development environments, and multi-threaded background workers. It is best suited for general-purpose applications, staging environments, and moderate web serving where occasional CPU sharing does not impact overall system stability.
description: Vultr vc2-8c-32gb is an x86_64 General Purpose virtual server from the Cloud Compute family, featuring 8 shared Intel vCPUs (4 physical cores, 2 threads per core) running at 2.0 GHz, paired with 32.0 GB of RAM and 640 GB of NVMe SSD storage. Benchmark data indicates a clear tradeoff between single-core and multi-core performance; single-core stress-ng and compression metrics fall in the bottom 10%, whereas multi-core stress-ng and PassMark CPU scores reach average tiers. Memory read bandwidth is stable, but large-block write speeds are weak. This shared-CPU instance provides a cost-effective option for workloads requiring high memory density and local storage without demanding continuous, dedicated single-core performance. It is best suited for general-purpose applications, multi-threaded background tasks, staging environments, and moderate Redis caching.

tagline: A shared-CPU virtual server offering balanced memory capacity and local NVMe storage for general-purpose workloads and staging environments.

640 GB local NVMe SSD storage included
Average multi-core performance balanced by lower single-core efficiency
